story:
The jewellery box waits at the back of the drawer. I open it and trace the shallow grooves of a golden map of Africa, the blue tanzanite marking Tanzania. As the pendant warms under my fingers, I’m back in the Zanzibar market where we bought it twenty years ago: the crush of heat and dust, narrow alleys teeming with people, the scent of cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves so strong it still carries me there; the mystery of that place seen through the haze of eyes in love. On the beach, with crystal water licking the white sand, he handed me an oblong blue velvet box. The chain glittered; the blue stone split sunlight into shards. He fastened it around my neck. “It looks great,” he said. I kissed him. We were certain our happiness would last. How young we were. What is youth for, if not for folly?

Back home, we floated on our small island of joy. I was sure I’d found the one and hoped he felt the same. At family weddings, the question shifted from “When is your turn, dear?” to “So when are you and David going to tie the knot?” Yet he lingered. He grew restless. When he booked dinner at The Grand, I knew—this was it. I prepared like a bride, shimmering under candlelight and lilies, hardly noticing the beautiful plates. I was prepared for it: a soft grey teddy called Edward, and the ring hidden in his pocket. He fidgeted, then said, “I wanted to talk to you about our future.” My heart leaped—and fell. “I’ve joined the army.” The world stopped. Air thinned. He had dreaded telling me, he said; he’d meant to in Zanzibar. “It’s not forever—just a few years. My grandfather was a soldier, so was my dad. It’s in my bones. I love you, and I will come back for you. Please wait for me.” I argued, pleaded, bargained, even threatened, but he was resolute. He had discovered his purpose.

I watched him walk away at the airport and cried for weeks. I wrote every day at first, then clung to his texts, his emails, the rare letters when they sent him to Afghanistan. Whenever the news mentioned it, time stalled; I prayed he was far from the blast. Gradually my storms calmed. I went out with friends, poured myself into work, relearned how to be alone. I tried on singleness like a coat I’d thought I’d outgrown.

Two summers later he returned, as promised. We met at the fair on the village green, sunlight rare and kind. He was taller, leaner, more certain than before. “What was it like?” “How have you been?” we asked at once, familiar and strange. We ate spun sugar and wandered the booths. At the target shoot he straightened with quiet pride. “I’ll win that teddy for you,” he said. I laughed—these things are rigged. He won anyway. The booth woman handed me a soft grey bear. “His name is Edward,” she said. “Look in his pocket,” David urged. My fingers found a ring. The game had been rigged. “I told you I’d come back for you.”

I stared at him, then down at the ring. How could I tell him I’d met someone else? I'd come to the fair to end it.

In the drawer, the tanzanite burns blue—a tiny country on a golden map, holding the heat and sweetness of a love that mistimed itself.
